2. Key observations

Stacked LOS displacement per track (USGS epicentre = yellow star). Below: quadtree-downsampled data / model / residual with the GBIS fault (black) and distributed-model surface-extended fault (gray).

Same tracks BEFORE stacking — a single coseismic interferogram. Compare the noisier single-pair field (turbulent atmosphere, decorrelation) with the stacked field above; the table quantifies the noise suppression.

Luo+21 stacking — acquisition periods & noise suppression

Trackpairs (pre+post)time range signal amp (cm)far-field σ (cm)
single → stack		 noise reductionSNR single → stack
t144_dsc6+620191112 → 202006154.10.83 → 0.661.26× (√N=2.5)4.9 → 6.2

Far-field σ is the robust (MAD) standard deviation of LOS in the signal-free outer ring — a proxy for residual atmospheric/decorrelation noise. Stacking N coseismic pairs averages down random atmosphere; the achieved reduction is below the ideal √N because long-wavelength tropospheric delay is spatially correlated and does not average as fast as white noise. SNR = signal amplitude / far-field σ.

t144_dsc

3. Source model (uniform Okada)

Focal mechanism vs USGS (left→right: USGS, uniform, slip-weighted). On each beachball the green solid curve is the chosen fault plane and the red-dashed curve its auxiliary (conjugate) plane. Parameter table: initial start · search bounds · optimum · posterior median (railed rows red).

USGS vs inverted — parameters & depth

parameterUSGS NP2 / hypopygbis invertedΔ (model−USGS)
Mw6.506.53+0.03
Strike (°)7380+6
Dip (°)7843-36
Rake (°)-24-15+9
Depth (km)2.7
hypocentre		6.0
top 3.6 · bottom 8.4		+3.3
centroid−hypo

Depth: GBIS fault depth is the up-dip (top) edge; centroid and bottom are derived from inverted Width & dip. The USGS depth is the hypocentre (nucleation), which typically sits near the fault bottom — so model bottom ≈ USGS hypo while the slip centroid is shallower.

5. Static stress drop

Average static stress drop. Left: uniform (Eshelby + geometric) vs distributed estimates. Right: Δσ vs smoothing — a flat plateau over the data-resolvable range means Δσ is data-constrained, not a smoothing artefact.

modelr_eq (km)area (km²)slip (m)Δσ (MPa)
uniform Okada (Eshelby r=√(LW/π))7.2164.11.479.03
uniform Okada (geometric μ·D/W)——1.476.73
distributed (slip≥0.1·max area)11.0377.11.536.22

Average static stress drop. Eshelby circular-crack Δσ=(7/16)·M0/r³ is the standard single estimate; geometric μ·D/W is a shape-dependent check. Typical tectonic earthquakes: 1–10 MPa (intraplate often higher).
